title: Trump announces political risk insurance for ships in Gulf
author: CBS News
fullText: Now, President Trump today posted to social media stating that he has ordered political risk insurance for the financial security of all maritime trade traveling through the Gulf, adding, quote, "The United States Navy will begin escorting tankers through the straight of Hormuz as soon as possible." I want to bring in CBS News money watch correspondent Kelly O'Grady, who has been monitoring what's happening there. Kelly, can you break down the message today from the president and will that be enough to entice oil tankers to cross that passage? >> Yeah, this is pretty significant that the president is not only offering the US Navy, as you mentioned, to essentially escort ships through the that straight of Hormuz. And we're not just talking oil. We're also talking general trade as well and gas. But also that risk insurance because earlier today uh we reported out that some of our sources were telling us from the oil industry that these shipping companies were being denied war risk coverage. And so essentially what that means is in in a normal situation, these shipping companies, they will have a special type of marine insurance that they can file a claim if their ship is hit or their goods are damaged due to piracy or terrorism or anything to do with war. And so with that being taken away that makes that journey through the state not only damage from uh you know a life ordeath perspective but also insuring those goods because if you are then responsible for that lost oil or whatever it might be that's incredibly costly from a financial perspective. So this is very significant. Now to your second question of whether I think that this will entice shipments or shipping companies to do so. I think the answer is yes. And and oil investors already are thinking that as well because we had Brent crude up around $84 a barrel. It was at at one point up 9% versus yesterday. We ended the day right around 81. So definitely there was a creep back down. Still oil was up for the day, but it shows that investors do think that this incentive may work. My question though is we've now done something to help with the actual transport of the oil supply, but what about the actual production? Because remember, Liz, this is not just about the straight of Hormuz. We are also seeing energy infrastructure in Saudi Arabia, in Qatar, in other nations in that Middle East being targeted. That's not something that's going away at this point. >> Right. And Kelly, I got to ask you about the Dow. Another dramatic day. Can you walk us through what happened and are investors, what are they most concerned about right now? Is it the strain of hormuz and oil production and distribution? Is it the actual war itself? >> My god, it was the roller coaster today. We were down at one point close to,300 points with the Dow. We finished right around 400. Kind of a sort of a little bit worse than average negative day, if you will, but certainly off those session lows. And the reason for these wild swings is partially oil prices, but it's what energy prices going up could mean for the economy. Because remember, when you have oil prices go up, you have gas prices go up, you have diesel prices go up eventually, and then that trickles through to inflation. And we have pretty much gotten for the most part inflation under control. And all of a sudden, investors are seeing this red flag of, oh gosh, what if inflation starts? what if we don't get interest rate cuts at all at this point? And it's sort of that that snowballing effect of anxiety. So, that's really what they were worried about. I think the fact that you saw the president come out and make this promise, this commitment to help ships through the straight of horn moves, it calms some fears. But tomorrow is another day. I think the bigger question is how long does this go on? How big does this conflict get? Remember when we talked about tariffs? What do the markets hate? Uncertainty. And we are right in the middle of a big old pile of uncertainty. No question about that. Kelly O'Grady, thank you so much.
